coredata.dc:title: Perylene diimide and g-C3N4 nanosheet as potential-resolved cathode luminophores for ultrasensitive ratiometric electrochemiluminescence immunosensor
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wei
Cui, Xiaomin
Li, Fenghua
Yan, Wei
Wang, Yunyun
Shang, Lei
Ma, Rongna
Jia, Liping
Li, Chuan
Wang, Huaisheng
coredata.dc:description: In electrochemiluminescence(ECL) field, the shortage of potential-resolved luminophores severiously limited the development of biosensors for accurate assay such as ratiometric biosensors. In this work, N,N′-dimethyl-3,4,9,10-perylenedicarboximide(PDI-CH3) and graphite-like carbon nitride(g-C3N4) nanosheet was firstly reported to produce potential-resolved ECL with coreactant K2S2O8 between 0 and − 1.2 V. And there was good overlap between the ECL spectrum of g-C3N4 and the absorption spectra of PDI-CH3. Moreover, PDI-CH3 and g-C3N4 were used to construct ECL ratiometric system for sandwiched immunosensors to detect tumor marker carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), in which g-C3N4 was used as ECL platform and PDI-CH3 was used as ECL label of secondary antibody(Ab2). Increasing the CEA concentration, the loading of PDI-CH3 on the electrode stepwisely increased, which leaded to gradual increase of ECLPDI-CH3 at − 0.5 V and significant decrease of ECLg-C3N4 at − 1.2 V due to the high quenching effects of PDI-CH3 to g-C3N4 through dual-quenching mechanism: competitive consumption of coreactant and ECL resonance energy transfer. And the ECLPDI-CH3/ECLg-C3N4 showed ultrasensitive linear response for CEA between 0.1 fg mL−1 and 1 ng mL−1 with detection limit 0.052 fg mL−1 according to IUPAC criterion. In addition, the proposed ratiometric biosensor exhibited good recovery in human serum samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Potential-resolved cathode luminophores
Perylene diimide
G-C3N4 nanosheet
Dual-quenching mechanism
Ratiometric electrochemiluminescence immunosensors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The ECL property of PDI-CH3 and g-C3N4
The ratiometric ECL detection mechanism of fabricated biosensor
Characterization of stepwise modification process of fabricated biosensor
Calibration curve for CEA ratiometric detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A. the ECL-potential plots of bare GCE(a), GCE/g-C3N4(b) and GCE/PDI-CH3(c); B. the ECL spectrum of g-C3N4 and PDI-CH3. C. The CV plots of bare GCE(a, c) and GCE/PDI-CH3(b and d) between 0 and − 1.8 V; a and b were performed in 0.1 M PBS solution, c and d were performed in 0.1 M PBS solution with 0.1 M K2S2O8. D. The UV–vis absorption spectroscopy of PDI-CH3 in aqueous solution(black line) and ECL spectrum of g-C3N4(green line).
The TEM image of GO/PDI-CH3(A), GO/PDI-CH3/AuNPs(B), g-C3N4 nanosheet(D), g-C3N4/AuNPs(E); C. the XPS of GO/PDI-CH3/AuNPs; F. The XPS of g-C3N4/AuNPs.

The EIS (A) and ECL-potential plots (B) of modified electrode: a. bare GCE; b. GCE/g-C3N4/AuNPs; c. GCE/g-C3N4/AuNPs/Ab1; d. GCE/g-C3N4/AuNPs/Ab1/BSA; e. GCE/g-C3N4/AuNPs/Ab1/BSA/CEA; f. The complete ratiometric immunosensor for CEA. C. the ECL-potential curves of constructed ratiomateric biosensor at different CCEA: a to i, from 0 fg mL−1 to 1 ng mL−1. D. The linear relationship of log(ECL1/ECL2) vs. logC CEA.

A. The molecule structure, ECL-potential curves of PDI-CH3 and g-C3N4, the ECL spectroscopy of g-C3N4 and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy of PDI-CH3; B. The preparation and detection of ratiometric immunosensor depending on dual-mechanism synergistically promoted sensing strategy.
The ratiometric ECL mechanism of fabricated biosensor.

head.ce:title: Perylene diimide and g-C3N4 nanosheet as potential-resolved cathode luminophores for ultrasensitive ratiometric electrochemiluminescence immunosensor

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review and editing
Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review and editing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry of Department, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, Shandong 252059, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry of Department, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, Shandong, 252059, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, 5625 Renmin Street, Changchun, Jilin 130022, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jining Ecological Environment Affairs Center, Jining, Shandong 272000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jining Ecological Environment Affairs Center, Jining, Shandong, 272000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In electrochemiluminescence(ECL) field, the shortage of potential-resolved luminophores severiously limited the development of biosensors for accurate assay such as ratiometric biosensors. In this work, N,N′-dimethyl-3,4,9,10-perylenedicarboximide(PDI-CH3) and graphite-like carbon nitride(g-C3N4) nanosheet was firstly reported to produce potential-resolved ECL with coreactant K2S2O8 between 0 and − 1.2 V. And there was good overlap between the ECL spectrum of g-C3N4 and the absorption spectra of PDI-CH3. Moreover, PDI-CH3 and g-C3N4 were used to construct ECL ratiometric system for sandwiched immunosensors to detect tumor marker carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), in which g-C3N4 was used as ECL platform and PDI-CH3 was used as ECL label of secondary antibody(Ab2). Increasing the CEA concentration, the loading of PDI-CH3 on the electrode stepwisely increased, which leaded to gradual increase of ECLPDI-CH3 at − 0.5 V and significant decrease of ECLg-C3N4 at − 1.2 V due to the high quenching effects of PDI-CH3 to g-C3N4 through dual-quenching mechanism: competitive consumption of coreactant and ECL resonance energy transfer. And the ECLPDI-CH3/ECLg-C3N4 showed ultrasensitive linear response for CEA between 0.1 fg mL−1 and 1 ng mL−1 with detection limit 0.052 fg mL−1 according to IUPAC criterion. In addition, the proposed ratiometric biosensor exhibited good recovery in human serum samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ratiometric biosensors [1–4] could eliminate the interference from instrument and environment and decrease false-positive events, thus caughting more and more eyes in accurate assay for early diagnosis and therapy [5]. Fluorescence [6,7] and electrochemistry [8–10] have been widely used to design ratiometric biosensors. Electrochemiluminescence(ECL), because of multiple advantages such as high sensitivity, low background and fast response, was also used to design ratiometric biosensors [11,12] such as dual-wavelength ratiometric biosensors based on spectrum-resolved emitters [13,14] and dual-potential ratiometric biosensors based on potential-resolved emitters [15].

Because dual-wavelength ratiometric biosensors required expensive spectrometer which caused high cost for samples assay, dual-potential ratiometric biosensors became more attractive. The potential-resolved emitters that could competitively consume coreactant or among which spectral overlap(the ECL spectrum of one luminophore and the absorption spectra of another luminophore) existed were used to design ratiometric ECL system. And the efficiency of ratiometric ECL system depends on the competitive consumption effect for coreactant and the extent of spectral overlap. Due to the shortage of potential-resolved luminophores, especially that with small ECL peak potential difference, cathode and anode luminophores [16,17] with large ECL peak potential difference were often used to design ratiometric system. For example, CdS quantum dots with ECL peak potential − 1.3 V and luminol with ECL peak potential 0.7 V that could competitively consume coreactant H2O2 [18] were the widely used cathode and anode luminophores in ratiometric system. The large ECL peak potential difference between cathode and anode luminophores made the weak competition for coreactant and low efficiency of ratiometric ECL system. Thus, some catalysts [19] such as noble catalyst [17] and hemin [20] were often introduced to enhance competitive consumption effects for coreactant and improve the efficiency of ratiometric ECL system. In addition, few potential-resolved luminophores showed strong spectra overlap [21,22]. Thus, some quenchers [23] such as Fe-based metal–organic framework [24] and Cu2O were often integrated with one luminophore to quench the ECL of another luminophore and improve the efficiency of in ratiometric ECL system [25]. Thus, to develop new potential-resolved luminophores in which strong competition for coreactant or strong spectra overlap exist was urgent for designing simple and highly efficient ECL ratiometric system.

For potential-resolved luminophores with the same coreactant and small ECL peak potential difference, the luminophore with low-trigging potential would largely consume coreactant and resulted in the shortage of coreactant for the luminophore with high-trigging potential when the potential scanned from low potential to high potential. Thus, to short the ECL peak potential difference of potential-resolved luminophores could enhance competitive consumption effects for coreactant. Graphite-like carbon nitride(g-C3N4) nanosheet was a widely used cathode luminophores with coreactant K2S2O8 at ca. − 1.2 V [26,27]. And the recently reported cathode luminophore perylene diimide derivatives(PDI) could emit strong ECL at ca. − 0.4 V with coreactant K2S2O8 [28,29]. The suitable ECL peak potential difference not only made g-C3N4 and PDI act as potential-resolved luminophores but also enhanced the competition for coreactant. Moreover, the g-C3N4 emitted ECL at 495 nm and PDI showed strong characteristic absorbance of perylene backbones between 400 and 600 nm [30]. Thus, strong spectra overlap existed between PDI and g-C3N4. It was possible to use PDI and g-C3N4 to construct highly efficient ECL ratiometric system for biosensors. Herein, N,N′-dimethyl-3,4,9,10-perylenedicarboximide(PDI-CH3) was chosen with g-C3N4 to fabricate a ratiometric ECL immunosensor( Scheme 1A). Gold nanoparticles(AuNPs) were in-situ reduced on g-C3N4 nanosheets and grafted on glassy carbon electrode(GCE) as ECL platform to immobilize primary antibody(Ab1)(Scheme 1B). To load more PDI-CH3, graphene oxide(GO) with large surface area and good π-π conjugation structure was used as carrier. To bind secondary antibody(Ab2) and enhance the conductivity of GO/PDI-CH3, citrate-reduced AuNPs were assembled on GO/PDI-CH3 as ECL materials(Scheme S1). Finally, tumor marker carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) was used as target protein model and Ab2 labeled ECL materials were stepwisely immobilized on ECL platform to fabricate sandwiched immunosensors(Scheme 1B). In the PBS solution including K2S2O8, the ECL from PDI-CH3 steadily increased with increasing the concentration of CEA and the ECL intensity from g-C3N4 gradually decreased due to the high quenching effect of PDI-CH3 through dual-quenching mechanism: competitive consumption of coreactant and ECL resonance energy transfer(ECL RET). The ECLPDI-CH3/ECLg-C3N4 was taken as ratiometric detection signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ECL property of PDI-CH3 and g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para: The ECL property of PDI-CH3 and g-C3N4 was explored through recording the ECL signals of the modification electrode GCE/PDI-CH3 and GCE/g-C3N4 between 0 and − 1.2 V. The bare GCE showed no ECL signal between 0 and − 1.2 V in PBS including K2S2O8 ( Fig. 1A, a). And the GCE/PDI-CH3 also showed no ECL signal between 0 and − 1.2 V in PBS without K2S2O8(Fig. S1). However, the GCE/PDI-CH3 showed strong cathode ECL at − 0.44 V(ECL1) and relatively weak anode ECL at − 0.35 V(ECL3) in reverse potential scanning process in PBS including K2S2O8 (Fig. 1A, c), which indicated the produced ECL from the coreaction of PDI-CH3 and K2S2O8. And the ECL wavelength was at 691 nm (Fig. 1B, red line), about 142 nm larger than that of fluorescence wavelength (549 nm) of PDI-CH3 in N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF)(Fig. S2A). The different structure of normal excited states and excimers could be used to illustrate the separation of 142 nm between ECL wavelength and fluorescence wavelength [31]. Because the energy the normal excited state emitted is higher than that the excimer emitted (549 nm vs. 691 nm). The fluorescence spectra should be caused by excited PDI-CH3 monomers and the ECL spectra by excited PDI-CH3 dimers [32]. In addition, perylene derivatives were reported to form a dimer through intermolecular π–π interaction in one crystal [33,34]. And the fluorescence wavelength of PDI-CH3 powder was focused at 682 nm(Fig. S2B), which was emitted by excited PDI-CH3 dimers. And the fluorescence wavelength of PDI-CH3 powder(682 nm) matched with the ECL wavelength(691 nm), demonstrating the ECL wavelenth at 691 nm was emitted by excited PDI-CH3 dimers [29].

And the electrochemistry property of PDI-CH3 and K2S2O8 was recorded to investigate the ECL mechanism of PDI-CH3. Bare GCE didn’t show redox peak between 0 and − 1.8 V in PBS (Fig. 1C, a). GCE/PDI-CH3 showed obvious redox peaks of PDI-CH3- the first electron oxidation peak (PDI-CH3 -·/PDI-CH3) at − 0.32 V and the second electron oxidation peak(PDI-CH3 2-·/PDI-CH3 -·) at − 0.56 V and reduction peak(PDI-CH3 -·/PDI-CH3 2-·) at − 0.79 V in PBS [34] (Fig. 1C, b). And S2O8 2- in PBS showed obvious reduction peak at − 1.42 V on bare GCE(Fig. 1C, c). The first electron reduction peak of PDI-CH3 largely increased and was found at − 0.47 V on GCE/PDI-CH3 in PBS with K2S2O8(Fig. 1C, d). That was due to the strong oxidation interaction between PDI-CH3 -· and S2O8 2- [35], which generated neutral PDI-CH3 and SO4 -·. SO4 -· would further react with PDI-CH3 -· and produce ECL. The possible ECL mechanism of PDI-CH3 with coreactant K2S2O8 [28,29,36,37] was shown in Eqs. (S1-S8).

The GCE/g-C3N4 showed strong cathode ECL at − 1.2 V(ECL2)(Fig. 1A, b) with wavelength 495 nm(Fig. 1B, black line) in PBS with K2S2O8. And obvious reduction peak of S2O8 2- at − 1.29 V was found on GCE/g-C3N4 in PBS with K2S2O8(Fig. S3, red line). The strong ECL on GCE/g-C3N4 should be from excited g-C3N4 (g-C3N4 *) produced by g-C3N4 -·and SO4 -·. The ECL mechanism of g-C3N4 with coreactant K2S2O8 [38] was shown in Eqs. (S9-S14).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ratiometric ECL detection mechanism of fabricated biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: In the PBS solution including 0.1 M S2O8 2−, the g-C3N4 and S2O8 2− would get electron from electrode and respectively became g-C3N4 -· and SO4 -·. Then g-C3N4 -· reacted with SO4 -· to generate excited g-C3N4, which produced ECL2 with wavelength 495 nm at − 1.2 V. The Ab2 labeled GO/PDI-CH3/AuNPs was able to specifically bind to the CEA, which quenched the ECL of g-C3N4. The PDI-CH3 in Ab2 labeled GO/PDI-CH3/AuNPs would get electron and was electrochemically reduced to PDI-CH3 -· according to Eq. (S1). K2S2O8 diffused onto the electrode surface and reacted with PDI-CH3 -· to produce SO4 2- and SO4 -· according to Eq. (S2). And SO4 -· was further consumed by PDI-CH3 to produce ECL1 according to Eqs. (S3 and S4) at ca. − 0.5 V. Thus the consumption of K2S2O8 by PDI-CH3 at ca. − 0.5 V leaded to the shortage of K2S2O8 for g-C3N4 at − 1.2 V and decreased the ECL2 intensity of g-C3N4 according to Eqs. (S10-S12). In addition, Fig. 1D showed the good overlap between the ECL spectrum of g-C3N4(495 nm)(blue line) and the UV–vis absorption spectroscopy of PDI-CH3(from 400 to 600 nm)(black line), which demonstrated the ECL RET from excited g-C3N4 to PDI-CH3 in ratiometric ECL immunosensor. Thus, the ECL2 of g-C3N4 was quenched in ratiometric ECL immunosensor through dual-quenching mechanism competitive consumption of coreactant and ECL RET( Scheme 2). Therefore, the increase of CEA would lead to the decreased ECL of g-C3N4 and increased ECL from PDI-CH3.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of GO/PDI-CH3 displayed strong absorbance of perylene backbone(457, 482, 573 nm)(Fig. S4B, green line) indicating PDI-CH3 assembled on GO nanosheet. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) of GO/PDI-CH3 showed the peak of GO at 10.45° and characteristic peaks of PDI-CH3 at 8.15°, 11.21°, 12.09°, 13.41°, 23.59°, 25.23°, 27.54°, 28.09°(Fig. S5 and Table S1). And the XRD of GO/PDI-CH3/AuNPs showed aforementioned characteristic peaks of PDI-CH3 and the characteristic peaks of AuNPs at 38.15°, 44.51°, 64.67°, 77.59°. Transmission electron microscope(TEM) showed GO nanosheet with full of wrinkels on the surface(Fig. S6A) and PDI-CH3 nano-rods with length of ca. 276 nm lying on the wrinkels of GO surface( Fig. 2A). Round AuNPs reduced by citrate showed strong absorbance at 520 nm(Fig. S4C) with diameter of 11.87 nm(Fig. S6B). After AuNPs were grafted on GO/PDI-CH3, round dots were found well dispersed on the surface of GO/PDI-CH3 in the TEM image of GO/PDI-CH3/AuNPs(Fig. 2B). And the N1s peak (400.0 eV) and Au4f peak (83.4 and 86.9 eV) in the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of GO/PDI-CH3/AuNPs also demonstrated PDI-CH3 and AuNPs immobilized on GO nanosheet(Fig. 2C).

The UV–vis absorption spectra of g-C3N4 nanosheet (Fig. S4A, black line) showed strong absorbance at 294 nm which was ascribed to the electron hopping from π to π * . After AuNPs were in-situ reduced on g-C3N4 nanosheet, the absorbance band at 294 nm red-shifted to 314 nm and obvious absorbance band of AuNPs was at 553 nm (Fig. S4A, red line) indicating larger AuNPs grafted on g-C3N4 nanosheet. The XRD of bulk g-C3N4 showed the peaks at 13.11° and 27.51°(Fig. S7A, black line). The two peaks largely decreased in the XRD of g-C3N4 nanosheets (Fig. S7A, red line). The XRD of g-C3N4/AuNPs showed the peak of g-C3N4 at 27.51° and peaks of AuNPs at 38.35° and 44.37°(Fig. S7A, green line). The TEM image showed g-C3N4 nanosheet with irregular surface and the size of ca. 150 nm (Fig. 2D). And AuNPs with diameter of 38.4 nm were anchored on g-C3N4 nanosheet(Fig. 2E and Fig. S7B, a). The element mapping image of g-C3N4/AuNPs clearly showed the distribution of C(Fig. S7B, b), N(Fig. S7B, c) and Au (Fig. S7B, d). In addition, the N1s peak(399 eV), Au 4 f peaks(84.2 and 87.9 eV), Au 4d peaks (334.9 and 353 eV) and Au 4p peak(545.7 eV) in the XPS of g-C3N4/AuNPs (Fig. 2F) demonstrated the immobilization of AuNPs on g-C3N4 nanosheets. The larger size and XPS peaks areas of AuNPs on g-C3N4 nanosheets than that of AuNPs on GO/PDI-CH3/AuNPs were attributed to the different preparation methods of AuNPs in two materials (The details were shown in supporting information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of stepwise modification process of fabricated biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy(EIS) was performed in PBS with K2[Fe(CN)6] and K3[Fe(CN)6] to characterize the stepwise modification of ratiometric biosensor. The plot a in Fig. 3A displayed the electron transfer resistance (R et) of 42 Ω for bare GCE. The plot b in Fig. 3A also showed a small R et value of 51 Ω for GCE/g-C3N4/AuNPs due to good conductance of g-C3N4/AuNPs. The plots c, d and e in Fig. 3A showed a stepwisely increased trend of R et value (1728, 2884 and 5412 Ω) when Ab1, BSA and CEA were respectively immobilized on GCE/g-C3N4/AuNPs. The increase of R et should be ascribed to the bad conductivity of protein biomolecules. After GO/PDI-CH3/AuNPs/Ab2/BSA captured on the above modified electrode, The plot f in Fig. 3A showed a largely decreased trend of R et value(3110 Ω), which could be illustrated with good conductivity of Ab2-labeled ECL materials. And the obvious trend of R et changes indicated the fabrication of biosensors.

The ECL behavior of every modified electrode in stepwise modification process of whole ratiometric biosensor was displayed in Fig. 3B. The plot a in Fig. 3B showed no ECL produced on bare GCE. The plot b in Fig. 3B showed strong ECL2 produced at − 1.2 V on GCE/g-C3N4/AuNPs. After Ab1 (Fig. 3B, c), BSA (Fig. 3B, d) and CEA (Fig. 3B, e) stepwisely anchored on the above electrode, the ECL2 correspondingly decreased because of bad conductivity of these proteins. When GO/PDI-CH3/AuNPs/Ab2/BSA was further captured on electrode, the ECL2 largely decreased and an obvious cathode ECL1 peak at − 0.5 V from PDI-CH3 was found(Fig. 3B, f). To investigate the influence of GO and AuNPs on the ECL2 of g-C3N4 at − 1.2 V, the ECL of GCE/GO/AuNPs in PBS with 0.1 M K2S2O8 was recorded and no ECL was found(Fig. S8A, red line) as that of bare GCE(Fig. S8A, black line) indicating GO/AuNPs couldn’t produce ECL with K2S2O8. And the ECL of inmunosensor GCE/g-C3N4/AuNPs/Ab1/CEA/Ab2/AuNPs/GO was also recorded(Fig. S8B, b, red line). Hardly no ECL change was found compared with the ECL of GCE/g-C3N4/AuNPs/Ab1/CEA(Fig. S8B, a, black line). Thus, the decreased ECL2 was due to the dual-quenching effects of immobilized PDI-CH3(Scheme 2). In the fabricated ratiometric immunosensor, the slight negative-shift(from −0.44 to −0.5 V) for the ECL1 peak was due to steric hindrance interaction of sandwiched form [39].

To prepare high efficient ratiometric immunosensor, some important experiment conditions for example incubation time, K2S2O8 concentration and pH of PBS were optimized. The results in Fig. S9 showed that incubation time 2 h, 0.1 M K2S2O8 and pH 7.4 was the optimized conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration curve for CEA ratiometric detection
ce:section.ce:para: The ECL of the fabricated ratiometric immunosensor at a series of standard CEA concentrations were recorded to obtain the working curve of CEA detection(Fig. 3C). Increasing the CEA concentration, the loading of GO/PDI-CH3/AuNPs/Ab2 on the electrode stepwisely increased, which leaded to the gradual increase of ECL1 from PDI-CH3 at − 0.5 V and the decrease of ECL2 from g-C3N4. Such a decrease was due to the dual-mechanism synergistical quenching effects of PDI-CH3 to g-C3N4(Scheme 2). The results showed the ECL1, ECL2 and log(ECL1/ECL2) respectively exhibited linear response with logC CEA between 0.1 fg mL−1 and 1 ng mL−1. Their regression equations were ECL1 = 4179.3 + 959.6x and correlation coefficient(R2) 0.996(Fig. S10A), ECL2 = 4317.1–1115.4x and R2 0.998(Fig. S10B) and log(ECL1/ECL2) = 0.03127 + 0.26487x and R2 0.999 (Fig. 3D). Compared with the R2 of single signal ECL1 and ECL2 regression equation the ratiometric signal displayed a better linear relationship with logC CEA. In addition, the ratiometric detection signal could eliminate the interference from instrument and environment. Thus, the ratiometric signal’s regression equation was used as working curve of CEA detection with detection limit 0.052 fg mL−1 (according to 3σ/m, σ was the standard deviation of the blank solution and m was the slope of the corresponding calibration curve) [40–42]. Compared with other ratiometric immunosensors [43–46], the proposed biosensor also exhibited ultrasensitive and wide linear relationship without introducing extra enhancers and quenchers(Table S2).

The ratios of two ECL signals for four different sensors at C CEA 1 ng mL−1 were recorded to explore reproducibility. The ratios were 7.41, 6.89, 7.25 and 7.05 respectively (Fig. S11) and their relative standard deviations(RSD) was 3.2%. The two ECL signals of ratiometric biosensor’s for continuous cycles were recorded to explore stability. Both ECL1 and ECL2 was almost kept constant during 6 cycle tests indicating good stability (Fig. S12A). The selectivity of ratiometric biosensor was explored through recording the ratios of two ECL signals for sensors incubated with target protein CEA and interferences such as glucose, uric acid, cholesterol, human serum albumin(HSA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), prostate specific antigen(PSA) and alpha-fetoprotein(AFP). The results showed the ECL1/ECL2 of sensors incubated with target protein CEA(1 ng mL−1) was 7.05, that of sensors incubated with above interferences at suitable concentrations were close to 0 as that of sensors in blank PBS solution and that of sensors incubated with the mixture of CEA and above interferences was 6.91 (Fig. S12B). The comparation results demonstrated the sensors’ selectivity was satisfactory.

The feasibility of sensors in real assays was verified by using standard addition method. The sensors were incubated with liver cancer’s serum samples diluted by 100 times with adding different C CEA(0, 1.0, 10.0 and 100.0 pg mL−1), then the ECL1/ECL2 were recorded. The detection concentration of CEA was calculated according to above working curve and shown in Table S3. The rate of recovery for CEA was in the range of 99.07 − 102.13%, indicating good application in real samples.

In this study, we reported a new pair of potential-resolved cathode luminophores PDI-CH3 and g-C3N4 with coreactant K2S2O8 between 0 and − 1.2 V. And there was good overlap between the ECL spectrum of g-C3N4 and the absorption spectra of PDI-CH3. Moreover, PDI-CH3 and g-C3N4 were used to construct dual-potential ECL ratiometric system for fabricating sandwiched immunosensors with g-C3N4/AuNPs as ECL platform and GO/PDI-CH3/AuNPs as Ab2-labeling ECL materials. And CEA was used as target protein. Based on the high quenching effect of PDI-CH3 on the ECL of g-C3N4 through competitive consumption of coreactant and ECL-RET in fabricated immunosensors, the proposed immunosensor exhibited ultrasensitive linear response for CEA between 0.1 fg mL−1 and 1 ng mL−1 with detection limit 0.052 fg mL−1 according to IUPAC criterion. In addition, the fabricated ratiometric immunosensor exhibited good recovery in liver cancer’s serum samples. The proposed potential-resolved cathode luminophores and designed ratiometric ECL system would accelerate the development of accurate assay.

Wei Zhang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Xiaomin Cui: Data curation, Validation. Fenghua Li: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review and editing. Wei Yan: Methodology, Investigation. Yunyun Wang: Data curation, Investigation. Lei Shang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation. Rongna Ma: Methodology, Writing – review and editing. Liping Jia: Methodology, Writing – review and editing. Chuan Li: Resources, Writing – review and editing. Huaisheng Wang: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2021QB080, ZR2017BB084), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21804063, 21427808).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132492.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemiluminescence ratiometry: a new approach to DNA biosensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.-R. Zhang, J.-J. Xu, H.-Y. Chen, Electrochemiluminescence Ratiometry: A New Approach to DNA Biosensing, Anal Chem, 85(2013) 5321–5325.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.-L. Huo, H.-J. Lu, J.-J. Xu, H. Zhou, H.-Y. Chen, Recent advances of ratiometric electrochemiluminescence biosensors, J Mater Chem B, 7(2019) 6469–6475.

Bian, et al., A label-free three potential ratiometric electrochemiluminescence immunosensor for cardiac troponin I based on N-(4-aminobutyl)-N-ethylisoluminol functionalized graphene quantum dots, Sensor Actuat B-Chem, 334(2021) 129628.

Y. Lv, S. Chen, Y. Shen, J. Ji, Q. Zhou, S. Liu, et al., Competitive Multiple-Mechanism-Driven Electrochemiluminescent Detection of 8-Hydroxy-2′-deoxyguanosine, J Am Chem Soc, 140(2018) 2801–2804.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Fang, S. Zhang, H. Dai, Y. Lin, An ultrasensitive ratiometric electrochemiluminescence immunosensor combining photothermal amplification for ovarian cancer marker detection, Biosens Bioelectron 146(2019) 111768.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Zhang, H. Dai, T. Wang, Y. Li, S. Zhang, G. Xu, et al., Ratiometric Electrochemiluminescent Immunoassay for Tumor Marker Regulated by Mesocrystals and Biomimetic Catalyst, Electrochim Acta, 196(2016) 565–571.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric electrochemiluminescent strategy regulated by electrocatalysis of palladium nanocluster for immunosensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Huang, J. Lei, Y. Cheng, H. Ju, Ratiometric electrochemiluminescent strategy regulated by electrocatalysis of palladium nanocluster for immunosensing, Biosens Bioelectron, 77(2016) 733–739.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Wei Zhang obtained her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2016. She is currently an associate professor at Liaocheng University. Her current research fields focus on the development of novel electrochemiluminescent material for biosensors.

Xiaomin Cui is currently studying for the master’s degree in the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaocheng University. Now her research interests focus on the development of biosensors.

Fenghua Li obtained her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2011. Her research interests involve synthesis of functional nanomaterials, chiral interface assembly, chiral molecular recognition, ion-selective sensors and gas sensors.

Yan Wei graduated from Liaocheng University in 2008 with a master's degree in analytical chemistry. Currently working in Jining Ecological Environment Affairs Center, she has been engaged in environmental protection work for a long time.
Yunyun Wang graduated from Liaocheng University in 2021 with a master's degree in analytical chemistry. She is currently a teacher in Gaotang NO.1 Middle School.

Lei Shang received his Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Wuhan University in 2015. He is currently a lecturer at Liaocheng University. His current research fields focus on the development of novel material and mechanism for biosensors.

Rongna Ma received her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Nanjing University in 2014, and she is currently an associate professor at Liaocheng University. Her research interests include electrochemical analysis, biosensors and bio-functional materials.

Liping Jia received her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Soochow University in 2013. She is currently a professor at Liaocheng University. Her current research fields focus on the development of novel biosensors.

Chuan Li received his Ph.D. degree in Chemical Engineering from Beijing Institute of Technology in 2018. He is currently a lecturer at Liaocheng University. His current research fields focus on the synthesis of novel electrochemiluminescent molecules for biosensors.

Huaisheng Wang, professor of chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaocheng University. He received his Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Nanjing University in 2002. His scientific interests cover electrochemistry, biosensors and new materials.
